plan.contextChunk:
consecutive frames. After a small time interval, $\Delta t$, the camera
will move, generating a displacement in the points with respect to the
camera coordinate system equal to:

$$\mathbf{P}_{t+\Delta t} = - \mathbf{T}_{\Delta t}  + \mathbf{R}_{\Delta t} \mathbf{P}_t
$$
where $\mathbf{T}_{\Delta t}$ is the camera translation and
$\mathbf{R}_{\Delta t}$ is the camera rotation that took place over that
time interval, $\Delta t$. The velocity of a 3D point with respect to
the camera will be:
$$
\dot{\mathbf{P}} = \frac{\mathbf{P}_{t+\Delta t} - \mathbf{P}_{t}}{\Delta t} = - \mathbf{V} + \frac{\mathbf{R}_{\Delta t} -\mathbf{I}}{\Delta t} \mathbf{P}_t
$${#eq-general_camera_motion_equation_full}

To derive the rotation, we consider the **Euler angles**
(@fig-yaw_pitch_roll) and decompose the rotation using rotations along
the three axes (yaw, pitch, roll):

![Rotation expressed by Euler angles (yaw, pitch, roll).](figures/optical_flow/yaw_pitch_roll.png){width="40%" #fig-yaw_pitch_roll}

Each angle measures the rotation along the camera-coordinate axes. Using
this representation of the rotation, the rotation matrix can be written
as: 
$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{R}_{\Delta t} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos \theta_Z &  \sin \theta_Z & 0\\
-\sin \theta_Z &  \cos \theta_Z & 0\\
0 &  0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos \theta_Y &  0 & -\sin \theta_Y\\
0 &  1 & 0\\
\sin \theta_Y &  0 & \cos \theta_Y

interactions.label: Yaw (θY)
interactions.teaches: Rotating the camera around the vertical Y-axis (yaw) shifts the 3D point P and its projection p horizontally across the image plane.

interactions.label: Pitch (θX)
interactions.teaches: Rotating the camera around the horizontal X-axis (pitch) shifts the 3D point P and its projection p vertically on the image plane.

demo_steps.title: Default Camera Frame
demo_steps.narration: The camera sits at the origin looking down the Z-axis. A 3D point P projects onto the image plane at p = (x, y) through similar triangles along the dashed line of sight.

demo_steps.title: Yaw Rotation (Horizontal)
demo_steps.narration: Rotating around the Y-axis (Yaw) by 25 degrees swings the point P horizontally. Notice how the projected point p slides primarily along the horizontal x-axis of the image plane.

demo_steps.title: Pitch Rotation (Vertical)
demo_steps.narration: Now try rotating around the X-axis (Pitch) by -20 degrees. The point P moves vertically, driving the projected point p up along the image plane's y-axis.

demo_steps.title: Combined 3D Rotation
demo_steps.narration: Combining both yaw and pitch moves the point diagonally. Every camera rotation generates a predictable, instantaneous 2D displacement of points in the image—the basis of rotational optical flow.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Oblique view looking slightly down and from the front-left, so the Z-axis projects horizontally to the right and the image plane's orientation is highly legible.
interactionPlan.view_reasoning: (1) The dominant baseline is the optical Z-axis, which runs horizontally left-to-right across the source image. (2) Since this Z-axis points directly right on the screen, the camera must be rotated around the Y-axis (azimuth) so we look side-on at the optical axis, placing azimuth near -35 degrees. (3) The vertical separation of the X and Y axes, along with the visible rectangular aspect of the tilted image plane, indicates a modest elevation of about 20 degrees above the XZ plane.

camera_view.view_notes: Maintains the classic textbook 3D perspective where the Z-axis goes right, Y-axis goes up, and X-axis projects diagonally upward and back.

inline_constraints.default_view: Matches the spatial layout of the diagram: origin on the left, optical axis going right, image plane in the middle, and 3D point P on the right.
inline_constraints.visible_ui: Two sliders for Yaw and Pitch aligned along the bottom or top edge, avoiding overlay on the central projection elements.
inline_constraints.explainers: Tooltips appear when hovering over the rotation arrows, point P, or projected point p to identify their corresponding mathematical labels.
inline_constraints.framing: Preserve comfortable margin on all sides to allow space for the floating coordinate labels.
inline_constraints.camera: Locked to the specified orthographic side-view to match the classic static textbook layout perfectly on initial load.

interactionPlan.notes: Render the point P and its projection p as linked elements. When the yaw/pitch sliders are dragged, apply the standard 3D rotation matrix to P, trace the projection ray from origin to P, compute the intersection point with the image plane, and update p's green coordinates (x, y) dynamically. Ensure the curved red rotation arrows remain anchored to their respective axes.

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure is presented from a coordinate-aligned perspective where the Z-axis is perfectly horizontal pointing to the right, and the Y-axis is perfectly vertical pointing upwards. The X-axis is drawn diagonally pointing up and to the right to illustrate 3D depth. The image plane is a flat tilted rectangle perpendicular to the Z-axis.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot is taken from a standard 3D perspective angle where the Y-axis is vertical, but the Z-axis points diagonally down-right and the X-axis points diagonally up-right. The image plane is tilted accordingly.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: The viewing angles are noticeably different: the source figure aligns the Z-axis horizontally, while the rendered figure tilts the Z-axis downwards at a significant angle, rotating the entire layout relative to the frame.

shape_comparison.rendered_shape: thick 3D ArrowHelpers with bulky cone heads

shape_comparison.source_shape: cyan circular dots with black borders
flat grey rectangle with thin border
shape_comparison.rendered_shape: flat pale cyan rectangle with black border

discrepancies: The camera angle is wrong: the Z-axis is tilted downwards instead of being perfectly horizontal.
Points P and p appear as solid black spheres rather than cyan dots with black borders.
Rotational arcs (Yaw, Pitch, Roll) are way too thick, using heavy 3D tubes instead of elegant thin curves.
Coordinate axes are bulky 3D ArrowHelpers instead of clean, thin lines.
The green coordinate labels 'x' and 'y' on the image plane are poorly positioned and overlap/clutter with the green arrows.
evaluation.notes: The mathematical projection concept is accurate, but the camera viewing angle is incorrect, and the 3D primitives are far too bulky compared to the clean 2D line style of the source image.
action_items: Adjust the camera view parameters (azimuth and elevation) so that the Z-axis lies perfectly horizontal, matching the source layout.
Reduce the thickness of the rotational arcs (TubeGeometry radius) and the axis lines to make them look like thin, elegant curves.
Fix the materials or geometries of points P and p so their cyan inner color is clearly visible instead of appearing completely black.
Reposition the 'x' and 'y' labels on the image plane to avoid overlapping with the green coordinate arrows.

feedback.rationale: The mathematical concept and interactivity are correct, but the execution needs refinement in camera placement, element styling (reducing the bulkiness of 3D lines/arrows), and minor label positioning.

Reposition the 'x' and 'y' labels on the image plane to avoid overlapping with the green coordinate arrows.

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure shows a camera coordinate system from an elevated, oblique perspective looking down-left. The Y-axis points vertically up, the Z-axis points to the right, and the X-axis points diagonally up-right, with the image plane perpendicular to the Z-axis in the middle-ground.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot matches this elevated orthographic perspective well, with the Y-axis pointing vertically, Z-axis pointing to the right, and X-axis pointing diagonally up-right.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: The viewing angles match closely, maintaining the correct spatial layout of the coordinate axes, image plane, and projection line.

shape_comparison.source_shape: cyan circular dots with black outlines

discrepancies: Severe label overlapping: 'Yaw', 'Pitch', 'Y', 'X', 'theta_Y', and 'theta_X' are tightly clustered and overlapping, making them illegible.
The cyan color of points P and p is not clearly visible in the rendered output, appearing as mostly solid black dots.
Green coordinate arrows on the image plane are very small and faint compared to the prominent green arrows in the source figure.
evaluation.notes: Excellent geometry and conceptual faithfulness, but ruined by severe label overlapping and oversized font sizes.
action_items: Significantly reduce the font size of the labels (especially Yaw, Pitch, Y, X, theta_Y, theta_X) to prevent overlaps.
Adjust the label offsets so that they are placed further away from their respective geometry targets.
Increase the size of the inner cyan sphere or use a Ring/Disk geometry for the outline to make the light-blue center of points P and p stand out as in the source.
Increase the size and thickness of the green coordinate arrows (x and y) on the image plane.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual model and geometry are highly accurate, but the implementation requires minor visual refinements such as adjusting label positioning/font sizes, point colors, and arrow thicknesses.

evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot shows the Y-axis pointing straight up, but the Z-axis points diagonally down and to the right, while the X-axis goes diagonally up and right, creating a skewed coordinate alignment compared to the original.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: The rendered view has an incorrect camera angle that skews the axes; specifically, the Z-axis should be completely horizontal to the right rather than pointing downwards.

Points P and p appear almost completely black instead of bright cyan with a thin outline.
The green coordinate arrows (x and y) on the image plane are extremely small and barely visible.

action_items: Adjust the camera position and target so the Z-axis lies horizontally to the right, matching the textbook's orthographic-like projection.
Reposition the rotation labels ('Pitch', 'Yaw', 'Roll') and their respective theta labels to eliminate overlap and place them near their correct axes.
Increase the size of the inner cyan spheres for points P and p so that they clearly appear cyan with a black outline.
Increase the length and thickness of the green coordinate arrows on the image plane to make them readable.

gpt-5.5.failure_modes_analysis: The scene includes the main axes, yaw/pitch/roll arcs, image plane, projection ray, P/p labels, and origin label, with functional yaw/pitch sliders and clickable elements. However, the initial view is rotated compared with the source: the Z axis slopes downward instead of being nearly horizontal, and the image plane/point layout is more compressed. The projected point p and green x/y coordinate arrows are much smaller and more central than in the reference, where p lies higher/right on the plane and the green arrows are prominent. The cyan-filled point markers in the source appear mostly as black dots in the render because the cyan inner spheres are hidden inside opaque black outer spheres.
gpt-5.5.failure_modes_reasoning: Camera-Wrong: the initial orientation does not match the source figure, especially the downward-slanting Z axis and altered plane/axis perspective. Scale-Wrong: the p position and green x/y coordinate arrows are noticeably undersized and misplaced relative to the plane compared with the source. Color-Wrong: the cyan centers of P and p are not visibly reproduced, leaving the markers effectively black instead of cyan with black outlines.
gpt-5.5.geometry_accuracy_analysis: The scene constructs real 3D geometry: a camera-coordinate origin sphere, X/Y/Z ArrowHelpers, red yaw/pitch/roll rotation arcs with arrowheads, a translucent image plane at finite depth, grid axes on that plane, 3D point P and projected point p, a dashed projection ray, drop lines, green image-coordinate arrows, and labels. The main spatial relationships are plausible: P lies beyond the image plane, the ray from the origin passes through p, and the axes/rotation rings are arranged around the appropriate coordinate directions; minor issues include the image plane being slightly off-center and some rotation arcs/labels appearing positioned for visual resemblance rather than exact geometric construction from a common coordinate frame.
gpt-5.5.geometry_accuracy_reasoning: All major elements are present as true unwarped 3D primitives with only minor alignment/proportion issues, matching the score-4 rubric.
gpt-5.5.interactivity_usability_analysis: The code includes OrbitControls for rotate/zoom, but these do not count as meaningful interactions. There are two visible range sliders, Yaw θY and Pitch θX, and both update the numeric readout and visibly move P, p, the projection ray, and the green image-coordinate arrows. A pointermove raycaster provides hover tooltips for many registered objects including the origin, axes, image plane, points, and rotation arcs; these should visibly show small tooltips. Click handlers are registered on many scene objects: several show explanatory popup messages via postMessage, and clicks on the origin, point P, yaw arc, and pitch arc also trigger guided demo steps with visible tweened motion and slider updates. gpt-5.5.concept_accuracy_analysis: The figure illustrates a pinhole camera coordinate frame with X/Y/Z axes, an image plane at fixed depth, a 3D point P projecting along a ray to image point p=(x,y), and Euler rotations yaw θY, pitch θX, and roll θZ. The labels and popups are mostly figure-specific and correctly describe perspective projection p=(fX/Z,fY/Z), yaw about Y, pitch about X, and roll about Z, but roll is not actually interactive and the X-axis popup incorrectly says the camera X-axis lies in the image plane rather than being parallel to the image-plane x direction.
